name: CNN v16
description: 16-layer Convolutional Neural Network for image classification. A standard architecture with convolutional blocks, batch normalization, and a classification head.

head.name: Classification Head
head.description: The classification head — outputs probability for each class. The number of neurons equals the number of classes in your dataset. Uses Softmax activation to convert logits to probabilities.

options.name: SGD
options.description: Stochastic Gradient Descent — the classic optimization algorithm. Updates weights based on the gradient of the loss function.
options.when_to_use: When you have a well-tuned learning rate schedule. Often used with momentum for better convergence.
options.consequences: Can be slow to converge. May get stuck in local minima without momentum. Requires careful learning rate tuning.

options.name: Adam
options.description: Adaptive Moment Estimation — adjusts learning rate individually for each parameter based on first and second moments of gradients.
options.when_to_use: Good default for most tasks. Works well without much learning rate tuning.
options.consequences: Can overfit on small datasets. Weight decay is not properly decoupled from gradient updates.

options.name: AdamW
options.description: Adam with decoupled weight decay — fixes Adam's incorrect weight decay implementation. Weight decay is applied directly to weights, not through gradients.
options.when_to_use: Best choice for most modern deep learning architectures. Default recommendation.
options.consequences: Slightly more computation than vanilla Adam. Weight decay value needs to be tuned.

options.name: RMSprop
options.description: Root Mean Square Propagation — divides learning rate by running average of gradient magnitudes. Adapts learning rate per parameter.
options.when_to_use: Originally designed for RNNs. Rarely used for CNNs but can work in specific scenarios.
options.consequences: Not the best choice for image classification. Can be unstable without proper hyperparameter tuning.

options.name: ReLU
options.description: Rectified Linear Unit — outputs max(0, x). The most common activation function in deep learning.
options.when_to_use: Default choice for most hidden layers. Fast computation, good gradient flow for positive values.
options.consequences: Dying ReLU problem — neurons can permanently output 0 if they get stuck in the negative region. About 50% of neurons may be inactive.

options.name: LeakyReLU
options.description: Like ReLU but allows a small negative slope (0.01 * x) for negative inputs. Prevents neurons from dying.
options.when_to_use: When you observe dying ReLU problems — many neurons outputting 0. Good alternative to standard ReLU.
options.consequences: Slightly more compute than ReLU. The negative slope value (0.01) is a hyperparameter that could be tuned.

options.name: SiLU (Swish)
options.description: Sigmoid Linear Unit — x * sigmoid(x). A smooth, non-monotonic function discovered by Google Brain.
options.when_to_use: Modern choice for image tasks. Used in EfficientNet and many recent architectures. Smooth gradient helps optimization.
options.consequences: More compute than ReLU due to sigmoid operation. Slightly less interpretable.

options.name: Mish
options.description: Self-regularized non-monotonic activation — x * tanh(softplus(x)). Discovered by Diganta Misra.
options.when_to_use: State-of-the-art for many image tasks. Used in YOLOv4 and other modern architectures.
options.consequences: More compute than ReLU and SiLU. Less widely studied, so less community knowledge about best practices.

options.name: GELU
options.description: Gaussian Error Linear Unit — x * Φ(x) where Φ is the CDF of standard normal distribution. Used in transformers.
options.when_to_use: Originally designed for transformers (BERT, GPT). Good for fine-tuning pretrained models that use GELU.
options.consequences: More compute than ReLU. May not provide benefits for simple CNN architectures.

options.name: Cross-Entropy Loss
options.description: Standard classification loss — measures the difference between predicted probability distribution and true labels. Combines LogSoftmax and NLLLoss.
options.when_to_use: Default for multi-class classification. Works well when classes are balanced.
options.consequences: Doesn't handle class imbalance well — model may bias toward majority classes. Sensitive to label noise.

options.name: Focal Loss
options.description: Down-weights easy examples and focuses on hard ones. Originally designed for object detection with extreme class imbalance.
options.when_to_use: When your dataset has significant class imbalance. Forces the model to focus on difficult examples.
options.consequences: More complex — requires tuning gamma and alpha parameters. May not help if classes are already balanced.

options.name: Label Smoothing
options.description: Prevents the model from becoming overconfident by softening the target distribution. Instead of [0,0,1,0], uses [0.025, 0.025, 0.925, 0.025].
options.when_to_use: When the model is overfitting or becoming overconfident in its predictions. Good for regularization.
options.consequences: Slightly reduces peak accuracy. The smoothing value (0.1) is a hyperparameter that needs tuning.

options.description: High learning rate — large steps in the optimization landscape.
options.when_to_use: Fine-tuning with pretrained weights when the pretrained features are very different from your target. Or with learning rate warmup.
options.consequences: May overshoot minima, causing unstable training. Loss may oscillate or diverge.

options.description: Medium learning rate — balanced step size.
options.when_to_use: Training from scratch with SGD. Good starting point for most CNN architectures.
options.consequences: Good balance between speed and stability. May need learning rate scheduling for best results.

options.description: Low learning rate — small, careful steps.
options.when_to_use: Default for Adam/AdamW optimizers. Safe choice for most scenarios.
options.consequences: Safe but may converge slowly. Good for fine-tuning pretrained models.

options.description: Very low learning rate — minimal changes per step.
options.when_to_use: Fine-tuning pretrained models. Final training stages when you want to refine without disrupting learned features.
options.consequences: Very slow convergence. May need many more epochs to reach good performance.

options.description: Small batch — processes 8 images per training step.
options.when_to_use: Limited GPU memory. Small datasets where you want more frequent weight updates.
options.consequences: Noisy gradients — each batch is less representative of the full dataset. Slower training per epoch but more updates.

options.description: Medium batch — processes 16 images per training step.
options.when_to_use: Good default for most GPUs (4-8GB VRAM). Balances gradient quality and memory usage.
options.consequences: Good balance. Works well with most learning rates.

options.description: Large batch — processes 32 images per training step.
options.when_to_use: Good GPU memory (8GB+). When you want smoother gradient estimates.
options.consequences: Smoother gradients but may generalize slightly worse. May need learning rate scaling.

options.description: Very large batch — processes 64 images per training step.
options.when_to_use: High GPU memory (12GB+). Multi-GPU setups.
options.consequences: Requires learning rate scaling (linear scaling rule). May need warmup. Can reduce generalization.

options.description: Huge batch — processes 128 images per training step.
options.when_to_use: Large-scale training with multiple GPUs. Research experiments.
options.consequences: Requires learning rate warmup and scaling. Significant GPU memory needed.

options.description: Quick training — 50 passes through the entire dataset.
options.when_to_use: Testing and debugging your training pipeline. Quick experiments to verify everything works.
options.consequences: May underfit — model doesn't see enough data to learn complex patterns. Good for iteration speed.

options.description: Standard training — 100 passes through the dataset.
options.when_to_use: Small to medium datasets. Good starting point for most classification tasks.
options.consequences: Reasonable training time. Model should converge for most simple tasks.

options.description: Long training — 200 passes through the dataset.
options.when_to_use: Medium to large datasets. When 100 epochs isn't enough for convergence.
options.consequences: Better convergence but longer training time. Watch for overfitting — use validation set.

options.description: Very long training — 500 passes through the dataset.
options.when_to_use: Large datasets with augmentation. When you need maximum performance.
options.consequences: Long training time. Strongly recommend using early stopping to prevent overfitting.

options.name: None
options.description: No data augmentation — only basic preprocessing (ToTensor + Normalize).
options.when_to_use: When your dataset is already large and diverse. Or when augmentation would hurt (e.g., medical images where orientation matters).
options.consequences: High risk of overfitting, especially on small datasets. Model memorizes training data instead of learning generalizable features.

options.name: Basic
options.description: Basic augmentation — random horizontal flip and small rotation (±10°). Simple but effective.
options.when_to_use: Small datasets where you need more diversity. General-purpose image classification.
options.consequences: May not help with orientation-sensitive data (e.g., text, digits). Limited diversity compared to advanced augmentation.

options.name: Advanced
options.code: train_transform = transforms.Compose([transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(p=0.5), transforms.RandomVerticalFlip(p=0.2), transforms.RandomRotation(15), transforms.ColorJitter(brightness=0.2, contrast=0.2, saturation=0.2, hue=0.1), transforms.RandomResizedCrop(224, scale=(0.8, 1.0)), transforms.ToTensor(), transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])])
options.description: Advanced augmentation — flip, rotation, color jitter, and random crop. Significantly increases training data diversity.
options.when_to_use: Medium datasets where basic augmentation isn't enough. When you need the model to be robust to visual variations.
options.consequences: May introduce unrealistic samples (e.g., wrong colors for medical images). Longer preprocessing time per image.

options.name: Custom
options.code:
# TODO: Define your own augmentation pipeline
train_transform = transforms.Compose([
    # Add your transforms here
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
])
options.description: Custom augmentation — define your own pipeline. Full control over what transforms to apply.
options.when_to_use: Advanced users who know exactly what augmentations their data needs. Domain-specific requirements.
options.consequences: Requires knowledge of torchvision transforms. Wrong choices can hurt performance.
